reasoning: Concise, patient-friendly summary produced based on the provided content.
output:
Pulmonary hypertension is high pressure in the arteries of the lungs, which makes the right side of the heart work harder. It can be idiopathic (unknown cause) or caused by another condition.

- Main causes and mechanism: Narrowing or stiffening of the small lung arteries; can be due to autoimmune diseases (like scleroderma or rheumatoid arthritis), congenital heart defects, blood clots in the lungs, heart or valve problems, HIV, long-term low oxygen, chronic lung disease, or certain medicines.
- Common symptoms: Shortness of breath with activity, fatigue, faster heart rate, leg swelling, chest pain or pressure, dizziness or fainting, and blue tint to lips/skin in more advanced cases.
- How it’s diagnosed: Physical exam, echocardiogram, and usually right-heart catheterization to measure pressures; other tests may include chest imaging, blood tests, lung function tests, and sleep studies.
- Treatment approach: There is no cure; treatment focuses on the underlying cause and relieving symptoms. Options include medications (oral, IV, or inhaled), blood thinners in some cases, supplemental oxygen, and lifestyle changes. Lung or heart-lung transplant may be considered if other treatments don’t help.
- Important caveats: IPAH (idiopathic) is more common in women; prognosis varies by cause and severity; diagnosis can take time; referral to a specialist center is often important; avoid pregnancy and high-altitude environments.
- Practical next step: See a clinician, ideally a cardiologist or pulmonary hypertension specialist, for evaluation and management; seek urgent care if you have chest pain or severe shortness of breath.

This information is general; individual risk and decisions depend on your situation and should be discussed with a qualified professional.
